items.q: What can line in a composition imply, even without representing an object?
items.correct: Movement, direction, emotion, and visual rhythm.
items.misconception: Only the outline of an object.
items.why: Students treat line as merely descriptive (an outline). In design, line is expressive - jagged lines suggest energy, curves softness, diagonals movement, even abstractly.

items.q: What is the rule of thirds?
items.correct: A guideline placing key elements along lines or intersections of a 3x3 grid.
items.misconception: A strict mathematical rule that must never be broken.
items.why: Students treat it as inviolable. It's a useful HEURISTIC for engaging compositions - many great works ignore it. Centred compositions can be powerful too; the rule is a starting point, not a law.

items.correct: Visual equilibrium achieved with different elements of equal visual weight on each side.
items.why: Students confuse asymmetry with imbalance. Asymmetrical balance is still BALANCE - achieved through unequal but visually weighted elements (e.g., a small dark shape balancing a large light one).

items.q: What does a spike against the right edge of the histogram show?
items.why: It's Highlight clipping. “Shadow clipping” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.random_wrong: Painting directly onto the photo layer
items.why: Correct answer: Using adjustment layers and masks. “Overwriting the original file” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many tonal control points does a basic Levels tool offer?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
